日本語
☰

Japan, à la carte

Chef-in-Residence Tasting Menu — April 2026
CIRE — Japan Chef-in-Residence Empowerment Association
scroll

This document is a menu. What you order is entirely up to you.

We've laid out locations across Japan. Each offers a fundamentally different food ecosystem, a different world of ingredients, a different cultural experience. Where you go, and how long you stay, is your call.

Category A lists three places where CIRE provides on-the-ground coordination — introductions to people, access to kitchens and producers, and accompaniment. Category B is a dining experience in Tokyo. Category C covers other notable regions worth knowing about.

All three Category A locations are in western Japan, yet each faces a completely different sea — the deep, cold Sea of Japan; the shallow, calm Seto Inland Sea; and the subtropical East China Sea. Mountain ranges act as climate walls, producing food cultures that share almost nothing in common.

CIRE's recommendation: all three Category A locations, with at least a week in each — that's the minimum to get to know the ingredients, the producers, and to build real relationships with the community.

Category A

Curated by CIRE

CIRE bridges you to local people, kitchens, and producers — and comes along for the ride.

— 01

Yunotsu, Shimane 温泉津、島根県

SEA OF JAPAN Google Maps 🌐 WATOWA
Japan's only established chef-in-residence. No foreign chef has been there yet.
WHAT THIS MEANS FOR CIRE

WATOWA is the strongest candidate site for a summer/autumn residency. Building a relationship with founder Masako Oumi and the community in April will significantly affect what's possible later. Jason being the first non-Japanese chef aligns with Oumi-san's original vision of "bringing chefs from around the world."

WATOWA (わとわ, "a circle connecting people") was founded in May 2021. It hosts chefs for one week to one month. Past residents have included chefs specialising in Middle Eastern, Chinese, Mexican, Italian, and French cuisines — many return multiple times. One chef relocated to Yunotsu permanently and opened a craft beer and burger shop.

Yunotsu is a town of fewer than 3,800 people. It's a component of the UNESCO World Heritage site "Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine" — the port that once shipped roughly a third of the world's silver in the 16th–17th centuries. The town grid is virtually identical to a 1692 map — almost unchanged for 330 years. No convenience stores. Two public bathhouses with 1,300 years of history (Yakushiyu and Motoyu) anchor the town centre. Every Saturday night, Iwami Kagura sacred dances are performed.

April is peak season — sea and mountain harvests overlap simultaneously. This convergence is rare even within Japan.

Blackthroat seaperch nodoguro ノドグロ
Rock oysters iwa-gaki 岩牡蠣
Horse mackerel donchicchi aji どんちっちアジ
Spanish mackerel sawara サワラ
Cherry blossom bream sakura-dai 桜鯛
Bamboo shoots takenoko 筍
Bracken fiddleheads warabi ワラビ
Angelica tree buds tara no me タラの芽
Red snow crab beni-zuwaigani 紅ズワイガニ
Dried flat seaweed ita-wakame 板わかめ
Wakabayashi Sake Brewery (in the hot-spring town, est. 1869) Brews "Kaishun" sake using onsen water and local rice. Young head brewer Ryoma Yamaguchi also makes experimental sparkling sake.
Takyo Abeke (Omori, 20 min by car) A 230-year-old samurai residence turned inn. Chef Onodera forages mountain vegetables, cooks rice over a wood-fired kamado, and serves hyper-local communal dinners. Home base for Gungendo and MeDu fermentation products.
Hamada Fishing Port (30 min by car) Major fishing port. WATOWA chefs regularly go out with local fishermen and source directly from the boats.
4–5 hours (flight + train)
7–17°C (mornings are cool)
WATOWA offers everything from dormitories to a whole house rental. Takyo Abeke (mentioned above) is also recommended.
Early to mid April
— 02

Bingo / Fukuyama, Hiroshima 備後/福山、広島県

SETO INLAND SEA Google Maps
Japan's "Mediterranean" — 20 times the seafood density of the actual Mediterranean. Small-lot, multi-species artisanal fishing.
WHAT THIS MEANS FOR CIRE

Little Setouchi (a licensed shared kitchen) is a realistic venue for a summer pop-up. Local coordinator Tanida-san can connect you with producers and facilities. There's no formal CIR programme in this area yet — starting one here would be genuinely pioneering.

The Seto Inland Sea averages just 38 metres deep (compare the Sea of Japan's 1,350 m). Over 600 rivers pour nutrient-rich freshwater into it, and four narrow straits create powerful tidal currents. The result: 20 times the seafood per square kilometre of the Mediterranean. The defining trait is "small-lot, many species" — muscular, tightly textured fish and shellfish caught in modest quantities by artisanal fishermen.

Where Shimane (Sea of Japan side) means deep-water large fish and preserved-food culture, Bingo (Seto Inland Sea side) is a world of citrus, olives, salt, and delicate coastal species. Same latitude, but the Chugoku Mountains act as a climate wall, producing fundamentally different food traditions.

Spanish mackerel sawara サワラ
Cherry blossom bream sakura-dai 桜鯛
Rockfish mebaru メバル
Mantis shrimp shako シャコ
Conger eel anago 穴子
Clams asari アサリ
Hiroshima oysters hiroshima-gaki 広島牡蠣
Setoda lemons setoda lemon 瀬戸田レモン

Hiroshima Prefecture produces 60% of Japan's oysters — more than the entire annual output of the United States.

Onomichi (20 min by train) Onomichi ramen (soy-based fish broth with pork back fat), ONOMICHI U2 (warehouse conversion complex), LOG (hotel by architect Bijoy Jain).
Tomonoura (30 min by bus) The inspiration for Miyazaki's Ponyo. A 380-year-old sea bream net fishing tradition (starts early May). Tai-meshi rice, Homeishu medicinal liqueur.
Teraoka Soy Sauce (Fukuyama, est. 1887) JAS-certified organic soy sauce aged three years in century-old wooden barrels. They grow their own soybeans and wheat.
Setoda / Ikuchijima Island (40 min by ferry) Produces 60% of Japan's lemons.
3.5 hours by Nozomi Shinkansen
Around 15°C, mostly sunny
Little Setouchi (licensed shared kitchen), &Q (dessert pizza and single-origin coffee café-gallery)
Early April
— 03

Hioki, Kagoshima 日置、鹿児島県

EAST CHINA SEA Google Maps
Subtropical, volcanic, and unlike anywhere else in Japan — the place where sweet potatoes built a civilisation.
WHAT THIS MEANS FOR CIRE (MOST STRATEGIC)

Kodaira Corporation's "Well-being Town" project is transforming the Yunomoto Onsen area, and a chef-in-residence fits naturally within that vision. CIRE's co-founder already has a relationship with Kodaira's CEO, and Kodaira's nationwide corporate network could scale CIR beyond a single programme.

Kagoshima is Japan's number-one producer of pork, beef, eel, and green tea. Sakurajima's volcanic ash soil (covering 50% of the prefecture) is unsuitable for rice but ideal for sweet potatoes — and sweet potatoes form the foundation of the entire food ecosystem: imo shochu distillation → spent shochu mash as kurobuta pig feed → the distinctive flavour of kurobuta pork.

Trade with the Ryukyu Kingdom, East China Sea fishing grounds, and a subtropical climate have produced a food culture fundamentally different from the Sea of Japan or the Seto Inland Sea.

Kurobuta pork kurobuta 黒豚
Kagoshima wagyu kuro-ushi 黒牛
Silver-stripe herring kibinago きびなご
Chicken sashimi tori-sashi 鶏刺し
First bonito of the season hatsu-gatsuo 初鰹
Imo shochu imo-shōchū 芋焼酎
Tsukihigai scallop ※ off-season tsukihi-gai 月日貝
First-flush green tea shincha 新茶

Kanosuke Distillery (in Hioki) is a craft whisky distillery with English tours. The prefecture has over 100 shochu distilleries producing around 2,000 varieties.

In 1598, Korean potters brought to Japan by feudal lords began a ceramic tradition that continues over 420 years later. Two lineages: white Satsuma (which caused a sensation at the 1867 Paris Exposition) and black Satsuma (everyday ware for common people). More than 10 kilns still operate today, led by the Chin Jukan kiln, now in its 15th generation.

Serving food on locally handmade vessels — an experience where 400 years of craft tradition and cooking intersect. You won't find this anywhere else.

3.5–4.5 hours (flight + bus/train)
Highs around 21°C, warm spring
Tsukihigai scallop season ends in March
Already finished (earliest in Japan)
Category B

Tokyo

CIRE members join you for a meal and make the introductions.

Aoyagi — The Art of Sea Bream

A Japanese restaurant founded in 1910, renowned as the definitive destination for sea bream — its chef, Hirohisa Koyama, is known simply as "the sea bream master." Originally from Tokushima, Koyama trained at Kitcho, Osaka's most storied kaiseki restaurant, then staged Japanese cuisine festivals at the Plaza Athénée and the Ritz in Paris in the 1990s. He is the only Japanese chef to have been awarded France's Ordre du Mérite Agricole at the rank of Officier. All seating is private rooms. Wild sea bream from the Naruto Straits in Tokushima is flown in daily. His signature "hegi-zukuri" (gossamer-thin sashimi slicing) draws out the essence of each ingredient through knife technique alone. April is cherry blossom bream season. A CIRE member will accompany you and arrange a chef-to-chef conversation.

🌐 Official site
Category C

Also on the Map

Regions CIRE has been researching. If any catch your eye, let's talk.

Goto Islands

Nagasaki — East China Sea

152 islands. Udon with a 1,000-year history, stretched with camellia oil. "Phantom wagyu" Goto beef. Camellia craft gin. Hidden Christian UNESCO sites. April: peak aori-ika squid season.

Google Maps 🌐 Serendip Hotel Goto

Minamisoma

Fukushima — Pacific Ocean

20 km from the nuclear plant. Since 2011, the food system has been rebuilt from zero. Rapeseed and sunflowers for soil remediation. The most powerful story of "food resilience" in modern Japan.

Google Maps 🌐 Inspiration Kitchen

Hida / Takayama

Gifu — Mountains

Winters hit −20°C. Hoba miso (grilled on magnolia leaves), pickled-vegetable steak, 1,300 years of cormorant fishing, A5 Hida beef. The pinnacle of preservation and fermentation culture.

Google Maps 🌐 Doyana

Kamiyama

Tokushima — Pacific Ocean

Food Hub Project has run a CIR since 2016. A New York chef stayed for a full year. Produces 24% of Japan's sudachi citrus. The most established international CIR model in Japan to learn from.

Google Maps 🌐 Week Kamiyama

Himi

Toyama — Sea of Japan (Toyama Bay)

A fishing town at the base of the Noto Peninsula, facing Toyama Bay — Japan's top yellowtail fishing ground. HOUSEHOLD, a seaside inn and gallery, has hosted chef-in-residence programmes. Stunning views of the Tateyama mountain range rising above the sea.

Google Maps 🌐 HOUSEHOLD

Aki

Kochi — Pacific Ocean

A castle town in eastern Kochi, birthplace of Mitsubishi founder Yataro Iwasaki. The guesthouse Kochinoya serves as a hub for travellers and locals, with access to Pacific ingredients: whitebait, yuzu, and winter-spring eggplant.

Google Maps 🌐 Kochinoya

On Visas

The April visit — no issue

US citizens can enter Japan visa-free for up to 90 days. An April visit works as cultural exchange and research. The assumption is that you won't be doing commercial cooking activities.

Summer/autumn residency — work in progress

A chef skills visa requires 10+ years of culinary experience, a sponsor organisation that operates a foreign-cuisine restaurant, an employment contract, and 2–6 months for processing.

CIRE is currently investigating whether it can serve as a sponsor. Honestly, the bar is high. We'll discuss the details in person.

The April visit stands on its own, independent of any visa questions. Come, see, meet people, build relationships. We'll figure out the summer together after that.

Next Steps

1 Read this menu and let us know which places interest you and how long you'd like to stay.
2 Based on your preferences, CIRE will begin coordinating with the local communities.
3 We'll set up a video call to work through the details together — dates, travel, accommodation, cost-sharing.
CIRE — Japan Chef-in-Residence Empowerment Association
